tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: In a world increasingly drawn into binary blocs, India’s refusal to designate “enemies” is a mark of strategic maturity, not weakness. India fights when necessary and engages when beneficial. In this context, language itself becomes a potent form of statecraft. 
Also, India’s published joint and land warfare doctrines emphasise integrated operations and threat management rather than the permanent “enemy” terminology, aligning with this broader strategic framing.
tweets.content:
In a world increasingly drawn into binary blocs, India’s refusal to designate “enemies” is a mark of strategic maturity, not weakness. India fights when necessary and engages when beneficial. In this context, language itself becomes a potent form of statecraft.
Also, India’s published joint and land warfare doctrines emphasise integrated operations and threat management rather than the permanent “enemy” terminology, aligning with this broader strategic framing.
tweets.author: @NetramDefence

tweets.title: R to @NetramDefence: From a psychological warfare perspective, labelling someone an enemy solidifies identity for both involved parties. India skillfully denies that legitimacy. Adversaries remain fluid, situational, and reversible—a subtle yet powerful tool for exerting pressure.
tweets.content: From a psychological warfare perspective, labelling someone an enemy solidifies identity for both involved parties. India skillfully denies that legitimacy. Adversaries remain fluid, situational, and reversible—a subtle yet powerful tool for exerting pressure.
tweets.author: @NetramDefence

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: The operational benefits are undeniable. By avoiding escalation rhetoric, India maintains a flexible stance. Using the term “proxy aggression” allows for targeting terror networks without officially declaring war.
 The emphasis on theaterisation and jointness thrives under this deliberate ambiguity, sidestepping any ideological fixation.
tweets.content:
The operational benefits are undeniable. By avoiding escalation rhetoric, India maintains a flexible stance. Using the term “proxy aggression” allows for targeting terror networks without officially declaring war.
The emphasis on theaterisation and jointness thrives under this deliberate ambiguity, sidestepping any ideological fixation.
tweets.author: @NetramDefence

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: Historically, the DNA of India's approach has been clear: inspired by the Gandhi–Nehru doctrine of no permanent enemies, only permanent interests. Even after betrayal, the principle of Panchsheel with China evolved rather than being abandoned.
 Today, India successfully de-hyphenates its relationships with Pakistan and China through exports like Rafales and effective military drills with QUAD.
tweets.content:
Historically, the DNA of India's approach has been clear: inspired by the Gandhi–Nehru doctrine of no permanent enemies, only permanent interests. Even after betrayal, the principle of Panchsheel with China evolved rather than being abandoned.
Today, India successfully de-hyphenates its relationships with Pakistan and China through exports like Rafales and effective military drills with QUAD.
tweets.author: @NetramDefence

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: India’s strategic autonomy shines through: it partners with QUAD while maintaining relations with Russia. While the US identifies China as India's “primary adversary,” India does not reciprocate. 
This linguistic restraint allows India to navigate multi-alignment in a bipolar world effectively.
tweets.content:
India’s strategic autonomy shines through: it partners with QUAD while maintaining relations with Russia. While the US identifies China as India's “primary adversary,” India does not reciprocate.
This linguistic restraint allows India to navigate multi-alignment in a bipolar world effectively.
tweets.author: @NetramDefence

tweets.title: R to @NetramDefence: When addressing Pakistan and China, the official language favours terms like “neighbouring adversary,” “collusive threat,” or direct naming—never “enemy.” Even Operation Sindoor (2025) referred to “multiple adversaries.” The underlying message is clear: We can strike today and negotiate tomorrow.
tweets.content: When addressing Pakistan and China, the official language favours terms like “neighbouring adversary,” “collusive threat,” or direct naming—never “enemy.” Even Operation Sindoor (2025) referred to “multiple adversaries.” The underlying message is clear: We can strike today and negotiate tomorrow.
tweets.author: @NetramDefence

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: India cleverly avoids the legal trap that comes with declaring an “enemy.” Interestingly, India does use the word ‘enemy’—but selectively. 
The Enemy Property Act (1968) applies the term in a narrow legal context involving wartime assets, while India’s strategic doctrine avoids turning that legal category into a permanent geopolitical identity.
tweets.content:
India cleverly avoids the legal trap that comes with declaring an “enemy.” Interestingly, India does use the word ‘enemy’—but selectively.
The Enemy Property Act (1968) applies the term in a narrow legal context involving wartime assets, while India’s strategic doctrine avoids turning that legal category into a permanent geopolitical identity.
tweets.author: @NetramDefence

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: In 2017, the Ministry of External Affairs, responding to an RTI, stated that India had not officially designated any nation as an ‘enemy’ or ‘hostile state’—a remarkable position for a nuclear power with multiple unresolved borders. 
The Land Warfare Doctrine (2018) consistently refers to “adversary,” never “enemy.” Phrases like “degrade adversary capability” and “punish aggression” empower operations like Balakot without committing India to a state of permanent hostility.
tweets.content:
In 2017, the Ministry of External Affairs, responding to an RTI, stated that India had not officially designated any nation as an ‘enemy’ or ‘hostile state’—a remarkable position for a nuclear power with multiple unresolved borders.
The Land Warfare Doctrine (2018) consistently refers to “adversary,” never “enemy.” Phrases like “degrade adversary capability” and “punish aggression” empower operations like Balakot without committing India to a state of permanent hostility.
tweets.author: @NetramDefence

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: A revealing insight from the MEA RTI (2017): “India has no enemies.” Even after significant conflicts in 1962, 1965, 1971, and Kargil, no formal enemy declaration has been made. 
This strategy allows India to engage in simultaneous conflict and diplomacy—illustrated by the projected $118B India–China trade in 2025, ongoing border talks, and nuclear restraint.
 Non-alignment has been effectively upgraded.

In a remarkable turn of events that marks one of the most dramatic comebacks in modern Indian military history, Brigadier Shrikant Prasad Purohit has assumed charge as Brigadier GS (Intelligence) at Headquarters South Western Command, Jaipur.

Once at the center of the 2008 Malegaon blast case, Purohit spent nearly nine years in jail and fought a legal battle that lasted close to 18 years. Throughout the ordeal, he maintained that his interactions with extremist elements were part of authorized Military Intelligence operations aimed at infiltrating and monitoring suspicious networks.

In July 2025, the NIA Special Court acquitted him and all other accused, concluding that the prosecution failed to prove the charges beyond reasonable doubt. Following his acquittal, the Indian Army restored his career progression, promoted him to Brigadier, and entrusted him with one of the most sensitive intelligence appointments in the force.

As Brigadier GS (Intelligence) at South Western Command, headquartered in Jaipur, he will oversee strategic intelligence assessments related to the western theatre, including Rajasthan’s key border districts such as Bikaner, Sri Ganganagar, Jaisalmer, Barmer, and adjoining sectors linked to Punjab and Gujarat. His role will involve monitoring Pakistani military deployments, drone intrusions, cross-border smuggling routes, terror networks, and emerging hybrid warfare threats.

For many in the defence community, this appointment symbolizes institutional confidence, professional vindication, and the extraordinary resilience of an officer who returned from one of the most controversial legal battles in India to occupy a coveted Military Intelligence position at the heart of the nation’s western security architecture.

R to @NetramDefence: A key feature of this collaboration is the “shared reserve model,” wherein Abu Dhabi National Oil Company (ADNOC) stores crude oil in India’s SPR facilities while retaining ownership, but India has the first right to access these reserves during emergencies.

 This mechanism allows India to strengthen its strategic reserves without fully bearing procurement costs. The agreement also expands storage beyond Mangalore to include Visakhapatnam and planned facilities at Chandikhol in Odisha, while exploring storage in the UAE itself, including Fujairah. 

In parallel, ADNOC has expanded its engagement with Indian Oil Corporation to explore long-term LPG supply and trading opportunities, building on an existing term contract since 2023.
